content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5642 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

January 6, 1984

Gordon M. Hart
1301 K Street
Modesto, CA 95354

Re: Advice Letter No. A-83-264

Dear Mr. Hart:

Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of
interests provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts, as
stated in your letter, are that you are a member of the City of
Modesto Planning Commission. You are also the president and
100 percent owner of an architectural and engineering firm in
the City of Modesto. Your letter described four situations, and
you asked how the Act's provisions apply to each:

Situation #1: The City Council hired a Southern California
architectural firm to provide preliminary studies and,
eventually, construction drawings and bid documents for a new
police administration facility. Due to your structural
engineering background, office location in Modesto and knowledge
of local building procedures and contractors, the Southern
California architect would like to hire your firm to perform the
structural engineering services on the police facility. You
stated that there are no zoning or planning matters involved in
this project. You abstained from participating in the review of
the capital improvement budget (CIP) in which the allocation of
money for the project was approved. Your question is now the
conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform
Act apply if you work on the project and are paid by the
architect.

Situation #2: The City Council, acting as a Redevelopment
Agency, is developing a convention and community center which
will cost over $50 million. Because a recent study suggested
that sufficient funding for the project may be unavailable, a
group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including
your firm, are considering putting together a similar project
using existing buildings. This will provide a less expensive,

Gordon M. Hart
January 6, 1984
Page 2


but similarly functioning, community center. If this
alternative is feasible, the City will be asked to fund the
group's design studies. The EIR on the City's existing plan was
reviewed by the Planning Commission while you were a member, but
all other decisions regarding the project's location and
property acquisition were made before you were a member. Your
question is whether your firm may participate in the feasibility
and design study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the
group.

Situation #3: Because of your background in design and
construction, the Planning Director and Planning Commission
chairman would like you to participate in preparing next year's
capital improvement plan (CIP). The Planning Commission's
deliberations result in recommendations to the City Council on
the various items in the CIP. Your question is whether your
participation in the hearings and recommendation process will
prevent your office from submitting proposals or being hired by
the City to do architectural or engineering work on any of the
CIP projects eventually adopted by the City Council.

Situation #4: A client retains your firm to design a
project that will require zoning or other City planning
approvals:

(a)    May a representative of your firm make the
presentation to the Planning Commission, assuming that you
disqualify yourself from the decision?

(b)    If your associate makes the presentation and there
is a split vote may you handle the appeal to the City
Council?

Situation #5: The Board of Zoning Adjustments (BZA) is
appointed by the City Council and its decisions are ratified by,
or appealed to, the Council. Your question is whether you can
present zoning or variance requests before the BZA.

DISCUSSION

Government Code Section 87100­1/ prohibits a local public
official from making, participating in the making, or in any way
attempting to use his official position to influence a

_________________________

1/    Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

Gordon M. Hart
January 6, 1984
Page 3


governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest. An official has a "financial interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

     (c) Any source of income . . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer . . . . or holds any
position of management.

                                    (Section 87103.)

     You have an investment interest in Gordon M. Hart, Inc., you
are an officer in the company, and it is a source of income to
you. In addition, because you have a 100 percent interest in
the firm, any income the firm is deemed to be income to you
personally. (Section 82030(a).)

     Situation #1: If you work as a subcontractor on the new
police administration facility, the contractor will become a
source of income to you once Gordon M. Hart, Inc. is promised,
or is paid $250 or more in fees. Under the facts provided, the
City of Modesto will not be a source of income to you. You will
be required to disqualify yourself from Planning Commission
decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial
effect on the contractor.2/ The potential for disqualification extends not only to decisions concerning the police

_____________________

2/ The phrase "material financial effect" is explained in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copy enclosed.

Gordon M. Hart
January 6, 1984
Page 4


administration facility, but to all decisions affecting the
contractor.

Situation #2: Your firm, as part of a joint venture, may
participate in, and be paid for, designing an alternative to the
City's planned community center. You stated, during a telephone
conversation, that the Planning Commission will not make or
participate in any decisions affecting whether the City pays
for, or adopts, your group's designs.3/

Situation #3: If you decide to participate in the
preparation of next year's CIP, this will not prevent your firm
from later working on projects authorized in the CIP. If your
firm does provide such services, the City will become a source
of income to you and the discussion contained in footnote 3,
will be applicable.

You will be prohibited from making, participating in the
making, or attempting to use your position to influence4/ any
decision concerning a particular project in the CIP if you know
or have reason to know that the decision will have a material
financial effect on Gordon M. Hart, Inc., or any of your sources
of income.5/ (See the enclosed copy of the Thorner opinion,

3/ Assuming that your firm has a 10 percent or greater
interest in the joint venture, the City will become a source of
income to you, personally, once it promises or pays sufficient
fees to the group such that your firm's pro rata share amounts to
$250 or more. If the City becomes a source of income to you, you
will not be required to disqualify yourself from decisions
affecting the City because the City is comprised of the public
generally and the "public generally" exception will apply. (See
the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.)

You will be required to report the City on your Statement of
Economic Interests if it pays or becomes obligated to pay
sufficient fees such that your firm's pro rata share amounts to
$10,000 or more.

4/ The definition of "making participating in the making,
or attempting to use your position to influence" a decision, is
contained in the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700. "Participating in the making" includes making certain
types of recommendations.

5/ If the City becomes a source of income to you, it is
exempt for the reason described in Situation #2, above.

Gordon M. Hart
January 6, 1984
Page 5


No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975, which discusses "foreseeability" in
this context.) If you determine that one or more of the items
contained in the CIP will require your disqualification, it is
possible that you may still be allowed to participate in the
rest of the CIP and you should contact us for more information.

Situation #4: If your firm is hired to design a project
which will require zoning or other City planning approvals, your
associate is free to make the presentation to the Planning
Commission. However, you must refrain from making,
participating in or attempting to use your official position to
influence the decision concerning the approval if the decision
will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Gordon M.
Hart, Inc., or one of your sources of income. (See 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18700.) "Using your position to influence" a
decision includes furthering or attempting to affect in any
manner any decision:

     (1) Within or before |your] ... agency; or

     (2) Before any agency which is appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of |your] ... agency.

I have included copies of the Harron and Thacher advice letters,
wnich discuss this phrase and which should be helpful to you.

     If your firm decides to appeal a Planning Commission
decision to the City Council, you will be free to appear on your
clients behalf. You will not be "using your official position
to influence" the Council's decision because the Council is not
an agency on which you sit, nor is it an agency that is subject
to your agency's budgetary or appointive control. (See 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18700(e)(1) and (2).)

Situation #5: Occasionally your clients have zoning or
variance requests before the BZA. The City Council, rather than
the Planning Commission, has appointive power over the BZA.
Assuming the Planning Commission does not have budgetary power
over the Board, you are free to represent your clients before
the BZA.

My advice is confined to the provisions of the Political
Reform Act. You should discuss the possible application of
Government Code Section 1090, or other statutory and common law
provisions, with your City Attorney or private counsel. If you

Gordon M. Hart
January 6, 1984
Page 6


have any questions concerning the Political Reform Act, please
feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh
Enclosures

GORDON M. HART INC.

Engineers & Architects

November 23, 1983

Fair Political Practice Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attn: Ms. Barbara Milman
Chief, Legal Division

Subject: Requested Legal Opinion
on Conflict of Interest

Dear Ms. Milman:

This letter is a result of a telephone conversation with Bob Leidich
this morning concerning potential conflicts of interest. In accordance
with government code 83114(d) I am herewith requesting a response to the
following questions.

As you can see from our letterhead, I am the president of a consulting
architectural and engineering firm in the City of Modesto. I also serve
as a member of the City of Modesto Planning Commission. I would appreciate a legal opinion on the following four potential conflicts of
interest.

1. The City Council has retained a southern California architectural
firm to provide preliminary studies and eventually will enter into a
contract to provide construction drawings and bid documents for a new
police administration facility. As a member of the Planning Commission I did not participate in the review of the capital improvement
budget (CIP) wherein the money for this project was allocated. No
other zoning or planning items are involved. Due to my structural
engineering background, office location in Modesto and knowledge of
local building procedures and contractors, the southern California
architect desires to hire our firm to perform structural engineering
services on the police facility.

Question: Does this constitute a conflict of interest due to the fact
the City of Modesto will be paying the architect who in turn will
eventually pay me?

Gordon M. Hart
1301 K Street, Modesto, CA 95354

Norman E Wilson
(209) 521-1143

2. The City Council is acting as a Redevelopment Agency for the purpose
of developing a future $50 million+ convention/community center.
Although the EIR was reviewed by the Planning Commission, all decisions pertaining to location and property acquisition were made
prior my appointment. A recent study by a consultant suggested a
negative conclusion concerning potential funding for this project. A
group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including our
firm, are considering putting together a similar project utilizing
existing buildings on a similar but identical site thus coming up
with a considerably less expensive but similarly functioning community center. If this potential turns out to be feasible the City will
be asked to fund the group for these design studies.

Question: Can our firm participate in this feasibility and design
study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the group?

3. As my professional background and experience all involves design and
construction, the Planning Director and Planning Commission chairman
desire that I participate in the CIP process next year. As you
probably are aware, deliberations on the CIP only result in a recommendation to the City Council concerning the various items in the
CIP.

Question: Does my participation in the hearings and recommendation
process preclude my office from proposing and/or being hired by the
City to do architectural or engineering work on any of the CIP projects eventually adopted by the City Council?

4. Question: When a client retained my firm to design a project which
will require zoning or other City planning type approvals, is there
any prohibition to a representative from my firm, i.e. another architect or engineer making the presentation to the Commission assuming
that I step down and do not participate in the decision?

Correlary Question: Assuming that my associate makes the presentation
before the Planning Commission (and I have stepped down as noted
above) and a split vote results and the matter is appealed to the
City Council, can I personally make this appeal?

Correlary Question: Do you know of any prohibition that would prevent
me from personally making zoning or variance type presentations for a
client before our Board of Zoning Adjustment (BZA)? The BZA is
appointed by the City Council and its decisions are either ratified
by or can be appealed to the City Council.

I appreciate the opportunity to pose these questions to you and apologize for the lengthy dissertation.  As you can see there is a common thread running through all of the questions involving a member of the Planning Commission either receiving City funds for City work or making presentations for City bodies.

Thanking you in advance for your consideration.

Sincerely,
GORDON M. HART, INC.

Gordon M. Hart
President

GMH/tc

sections.conclusion: Situation #1: You will be required to disqualify yourself from Planning Commission decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the contractor. Situation #2: Your firm, as part of a joint venture, may participate in, and be paid for, designing an alternative to the City's planned community center. Situation #3: Participating in CIP preparation will not prevent your firm from later working on projects authorized in the CIP, but you will be prohibited from influencing decisions concerning particular projects if your firm is interested. Situation #4: Your associate is free to make the presentation to the Planning Commission. You may appear on your client's behalf before the City Council if your firm appeals a Planning Commission decision. Situation #5: You are free to represent your clients before the BZA, assuming the Planning Commission does not have budgetary power over the Board.

Enclosures
sections.question_synthetic: A Planning Commission member who owns an architectural and engineering firm seeks advice on five situations involving potential conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act: working as a subcontractor on a city project, participating in an alternative development proposal, helping prepare the capital improvement plan, having a firm representative present projects to the Planning Commission, and representing clients before the Board of Zoning Adjustments.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the Planning Commission member must disqualify himself from decisions affecting his firm's financial interests, but may work on most projects if he avoids using his official position to influence relevant decisions and properly disqualifies himself when necessary. The analysis distinguishes between decisions where disqualification is required and situations where participation is permitted, with specific guidance on appeals and presentations before bodies outside his direct control.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated November 23, 1983) and the FPPC response advice letter (dated January 6, 1984). The advice letter is comprehensive with five distinct situations analyzed with specific conclusions for each. Letter includes multiple footnotes with references to Government Code sections and enclosed regulatory materials. Minor OCR irregularities noted (e.g., 'oisqualify' for 'disqualify') but do not impair comprehension. Parsed as a single advice letter document due to the continuous nature of the analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does this constitute a conflict of interest due to the fact 
the City of Modesto will be paying the architect who in turn will 
eventually pay me? 
Gordon M. Hart 
Norman E Wilson 
1301 K Street, Modesto, CA 95354 
(209) 521-1 143 


2. The City Council is acting as a Redevelopment Agency for the purpose 
of developing a future $50 million+ convention/community center. 
Although the EIR was reviewed by the Planning Commission, all dec~ 
isions pertaining to location and property acquisition were made 
prior to my appointment. 
A recent study by a consultant suggested a 
negative conclusion concerning potential funding for this project. 
A 
group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including our 
firm, are considering putting together a similar project utilizing 
existing buildings on a similar but identical site thus coming up 
with a considerably less expensive but similarly functioning communi-
ty center. If this potential turns out to be feasible the City will 
be asked to fund the group for these design studies. 
Question: Can our firm participate in this feasibility and design 
study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the group? 
3. As my professional background and experience all involves design and 
construction, the Planning Director and Planning Commission chairman 
desire that I participate in the CIP process next year. 
As you 
probably are aware, deliberations on the CIP only result in a recom-
mendation to the City Council concerning the various items in the 
CIP. 
Question: Does my participation in the hearings and recommendation 
process preclude my office from proposing and/or being hired by the 
City to do architectural or engineering work on any of the CIP pro-
jects eventually adopted by the City Council? 
4. Question: When a client retained my firm to design a project which 
will require zoning or other City planning type approvals, is there 
any prohibition to a representative from my firm, i.e. another archi-
tect or engineer making the presentation to the Commission assuming 
that I step down and do not participate in the decision? 
Correlary Question: Assuming that my associate makes the presentation 
before the Planning Commission (and I have stepped down as noted 
above) and a split vote results and the matter is appealed to the 
City Council, can I personally make this appeal? 
Correlary Question: Do you know of any prohibition that would prevent 
me from personally making zoning or variance type presentations for a 
client before our Board of Zoning Adjustment (BZA)? 
The BZA is 
appointed by the City Council and its decisions are either ratified 
by or can be appealed to the City Council. 


-
I appreciate the opportunity to pose these questions to you and 
apolo~ 
gize for the lengthy dissertation. 
As you can see there is a common 
thread running through all of the questions involving a member of the 
Planning Commission either receiving City funds for City work or making 
presentations for City bodies. 
Thanking you in advance for your consideration.

CONCLUSION: Situation #1: You will be required to disqualify yourself from Planning Commission decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the contractor. Situation #2: Your firm, as part of a joint venture, may participate in, and be paid for, designing an alternative to the City's planned community center. Situation #3: Participating in CIP preparation will not prevent your firm from later working on projects authorized in the CIP, but you will be prohibited from influencing decisions concerning particular projects if your firm is interested. Situation #4: Your associate is free to make the presentation to the Planning Commission. You may appear on your client's behalf before the City Council if your firm appeals a Planning Commission decision. Situation #5: You are free to represent your clients before the BZA, assuming the Planning Commission does not have budgetary power over the Board.

Hart 1301 K Street Modesto, CA 95354 Re: Advice Letter No. A-83-264 Dear Mr. Hart: Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts, as stated in your letter, are that you are a member of the City of Modesto Planning Commission. You are also the president and 100 percent owner of an architectural and engineering firm in the City of Modesto. Your letter described four situations, and you asked how the Act's provisions apply to each: Situation #1: The City Council hired a Southern California architectural firm to provide preliminary studies and, eventually, construction drawings and bid documents for a new police administration facility. Due to your structural engineering background, office location in Modesto and knowledge of local building procedures and contractors, the Southern California architect would like to hire your firm to perform the structural engineering services on the police facility. You stated that there are no zoning or planning matters involved in this project. You abstained from participating in the review of the capital improvement budget (CIP) in which the allocation of money for the project was approved. Your question is now the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act apply if you work on the project and are paid by the architect. Situation #2: The City Council, acting as a Redevelopment Agency, is developing a convention and community center which will cost over $50 million. Because a recent study suggested that sufficient funding for the project may be unavailable, a group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including your firm, are considering putting together a similar project using existing buildings. This will provide a less expensive, Gordon M. Hart January 6, 1984 Page 2 but similarly functioning, community center. If this alternative is feasible, the City will be asked to fund the group's design studies. The EIR on the City's existing plan was reviewed by the Planning Commission while you were a member, but all other decisions regarding the project's location and property acquisition were made before you were a member. Your question is whether your firm may participate in the feasibility and design study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the group. Situation #3: Because of your background in design and construction, the Planning Director and Planning Commission chairman would like you to participate in preparing next year's capital improvement plan (CIP). The Planning Commission's deliberations result in recommendations to the City Council on the various items in the CIP. Your question is whether your participation in the hearings and recommendation process will prevent your office from submitting proposals or being hired by the City to do architectural or engineering work on any of the CIP
embedding.summary: The FPPC provides comprehensive conflict of interest advice to Gordon M. Hart, a Modesto Planning Commission member and 100% owner of an architectural and engineering firm, addressing five specific scenarios involving potential business opportunities with the City. The letter analyzes each situation under Political Reform Act provisions and provides detailed guidance on when disqualification is required and when business activities are permissible.
